evals.prompt: Review this RFP and extract the mandatory requirements and evaluation criteria. Here are the key sections: MANDATORY REQUIREMENTS: (1) Solution must integrate with Salesforce CRM, (2) Team must have 3+ years of experience in digital transformation, (3) Proposal must include specific examples of past performance. EVALUATION CRITERIA: (Weight) Technical approach (40%), Team qualifications (30%), Price (20%), Schedule feasibility (10%). We've never done a Salesforce integration before. Is this a blocker?
evals.expected_output: Should identify Salesforce integration as a mandatory requirement (blocker unless we can address it), extract evaluation weightings showing technical approach is most heavily weighted, and flag the team experience requirement as high-risk since the firm lacks demonstrated Salesforce integration experience. Should recommend either partnering with a Salesforce specialist, disclosing the gap with a mitigation plan, or declining the RFP if risk is unacceptable.

evals.prompt: Build a compliance matrix for this RFP. The RFP has 8 requirements: 1) Implement project management system, 2) Migrate 500+ documents, 3) Train 50 users, 4) Provide 60 days of post-launch support, 5) Document all processes, 6) Integrate with existing ERP system, 7) Ensure GDPR compliance, 8) Achieve 99% system uptime. Our proposal sections are: Executive Summary, Technical Approach, Team Experience, Timeline, and Assumptions. Map each requirement to a proposal section.
evals.expected_output: Should create a matrix showing each RFP requirement mapped to proposal section(s) where it will be addressed. Should note that GDPR compliance and system uptime are likely outside consulting firm scope (flag for client clarification). Should recommend dedicated subsections for migration scope, training plan, support terms, and integration approach. Should identify where assumptions are critical (e.g., 99% uptime assumes infrastructure beyond consulting scope).

evals.prompt: Rewrite this proposal section to improve clarity and scoring potential. Here's what we wrote: 'Our approach combines strategic thinking with practical execution. We bring deep expertise in organizational change management and leverage best practices from similar engagements. Our team will work collaboratively with your stakeholders to ensure adoption and maximize ROI.' The RFP evaluates on: clear methodology, specific deliverables, and team qualifications.
evals.expected_output: Should rewrite to be more specific and scannable: Replace 'deep expertise' with concrete evidence, define 'organizational change management' with actual steps (e.g., stakeholder mapping, communication plan, training design), replace 'similar engagements' with specific past performance example, add clear deliverables (e.g., change readiness assessment, communication strategy document, training materials), and structure with headers and bullets for easy scoring. Example: 'Our change management approach includes: 1) Stakeholder analysis and readiness assessment, 2) Change impact communication plan, 3) User training program, 4) Adoption metrics dashboard.'

evals.prompt: Identify legal or commercial risk in this draft response: 'We will ensure the system is fully optimized and performs to the highest industry standards. All requested features will be implemented without limitation. We guarantee customer satisfaction and commit to unlimited revisions until you are completely satisfied with the solution. Post-launch support will be provided as needed for as long as required.'
evals.expected_output: Should flag multiple high-risk statements: 'ensure...fully optimized' and 'highest industry standards' are undefined and unmeasurable; 'all requested features...without limitation' creates unbounded scope; 'guarantee customer satisfaction' and 'unlimited revisions until completely satisfied' are impossible to deliver and create unlimited liability; 'post-launch support as needed for as long as required' is an indefinite commitment with no budget protection. Should recommend replacing with: 'We will optimize performance per agreed success metrics, implement defined feature set per scope, meet acceptance criteria per Section X, provide up to 3 revision rounds, and post-launch support for 30 days at 40 hours/month.'

evals.prompt: Help me develop a win theme for this RFP. Context: We're responding to a government agency RFP for a 3-year digital transformation program. The buyer's primary stated goals are: (1) reduce administrative burden on staff, (2) improve citizen experience with digital services, (3) ensure compliance with new data privacy regulations. Competitors likely include: larger consulting firms (Deloitte, Accenture) with more staff, and smaller specialized firms with deeper government experience. Our firm: mid-size, strong in UX/citizen experience, decent compliance experience, but lighter on government program management.
evals.expected_output: Should develop a win theme that positions the firm's UX/citizen experience strength as the differentiator (something larger firms often miss), positions mid-size as advantage (more nimble than mega-firms, more staff than boutiques), and addresses the compliance requirement credibly without overstating. Example win theme: 'Citizen-First Transformation: We put citizens' digital experience first, reducing burden through intuitive design while ensuring compliance. Our mid-size firm combines UX/design expertise with government compliance knowledge, delivering more innovation per dollar than enterprise consultancies.' Should also recommend positioning smaller competitors as limited in scope, and Deloitte/Accenture as generalists who may over-engineer the solution.

evals.prompt: Draft an executive summary (one paragraph, 5-7 sentences) that demonstrates understanding of the buyer's challenge and positions our approach without overpromising. The RFP is from a mid-market software company whose primary challenge is: they're growing fast but their onboarding experience is causing 35% of free users to churn before completing first success. They want to improve onboarding while maintaining pricing and freemium model.
evals.expected_output: Should write an executive summary that: (1) Demonstrates understanding of the specific challenge (35% churn, tied to onboarding, threatens growth), (2) names the approach clearly (e.g., 'streamlined, goal-based onboarding design'), (3) positions differentiator aligned to evaluation criteria (e.g., 'UX expertise,' 'rapid iteration approach'), (4) avoids absolute claims or overprices ('will improve retention by X%'), (5) is concise and scannable. Example: 'Your onboarding experience is a bottleneck to growth. We'll redesign it as a guided, goal-based journey that gets users to first success faster, reducing churn while maintaining your freemium model. Our approach combines user research, rapid design iteration, and A/B testing, delivering a new onboarding experience in 8 weeks. We've used this method to reduce onboarding churn by 20-30% across similar SaaS products.'

evals.prompt: Develop clarification questions before we submit our proposal. The RFP says: 'Solution must provide comprehensive end-to-end visibility across all business processes.' But we don't know what 'all business processes' means. The buyer is a healthcare provider with potentially complex workflows. What clarification questions should we ask?
evals.expected_output: Should ask specific questions that narrow scope ambiguity: 'Which business processes are in scope? [e.g., patient intake, clinical workflows, billing, HR, supply chain]?' 'What level of visibility is required? [e.g., real-time dashboards, historical reporting, compliance-focused, performance analytics]?' 'Does end-to-end include third-party services, or only internal processes?' 'Which existing systems need to feed into this visibility?' 'Are there regulatory requirements (HIPAA, etc.) that constrain what's visible to whom?' 'Is this a single dashboard, or multiple tools per role?' These questions will help scope the actual work before committing in the proposal.

evals.prompt: We won an RFP and just kicked off the engagement. The proposal promised a 6-month delivery timeline with 2 major milestones. We're now a month in and it's clear the scope is bigger than we thought. What should we do immediately to prevent delivery risk?
evals.expected_output: Should recommend: (1) Schedule a structured scope review with the client - clarify which promised deliverables are top priority vs. nice-to-have, (2) identify if timeline compression or resource increase is possible, (3) prepare for a change order conversation if scope is genuinely larger than proposed, (4) document actual vs. promised scope to quantify the gap, (5) be proactive about timeline risk - communicate early rather than late, (6) review the proposal to identify where assumptions may have been missed (client responsibilities, third-party dependencies, access/data requirements). Should NOT attempt to absorb significant scope expansion without renegotiating - that's a path to unprofitable delivery. Recommend transparency with client: 'We've discovered scope complexity we want to address upfront. Here are options: [descope, timeline extension, resource increase, change order].' Framing it as proactive management rather than failure builds trust.
